content.title: Ansar — The Standard of Love No One Could Mat
content.subtitle: They gave everything they had — with a smile.
content.body:
Could you give away half of your home to a brother of Islam?

Or perhaps half of your lush orchard? Or sign over 50% of your business shares? While many of us might hesitate, fourteen centuries ago, a group of people in Madinah did exactly that—and they did it with smiles on their faces. They were the Ansar.

When the Muslims of Makkah arrived in Madinah, having lost everything—homeless and hungry—the Ansar welcomed them as if they were their own blood.

Sa'd ibn al-Rabi' (ra) said to his Muhajir brother, 'Abdur Rahman: "Half of my wealth is yours. Half of my garden is yours. I have two wives—choose the one you prefer, and I will divorce her so you may marry her."

Sunan an-Nasa'i: 3388, Musnad Ahmad: 13816)

History is full of hospitality, but no one ever turned their own comfort into someone else's right quite like the Ansar. Witnessing this ultimate sacrifice, Allah ﷻ revealed in the Quran:

"...and they give them preference over themselves, even though they were in need."

(Surah Al-Hashr, Ayah 9)

After the Conquest of Makkah, while everyone rejoiced, a wave of sadness hit the Ansar. They worried:

"The Messenger of Allah ﷺ has returned to his birthplace. Will he stay here now? Will he no longer return to Madinah with us? Have we lost his companionship?"

When the Prophet ﷺ sensed their fear, he comforted them with a promise of eternal love: he would remain with the Ansar in Madinah until his passing.

What a profound love! While the world measures wealth in gold, the Ansar measured it in devotion. To this day, the Ansar remain more than just historical figures; they are a beautiful poem written in the language of mercy.
